text: This quarterly progress report of the Aircraft Nuclear Propulsion Project at ORNL records the technical progress of the research on circulating-fuel reactors and all other ANP research at the Laboratory under its Contract W-7405-eng-26.
The ANP Project is comprised of about 510 technical and scientific personnel engaged in many phases of research directed toward the achievement of nuclear propulsion of aircraft.
The ART is to be a power plant system that will include a 60-Mw circulating-fuel reflector-moderated reactor and adequate means for heat disposal.

Package 1 includes, in addition to the cell, the major modifications of Building 7503.

The fuel concentration in the room-temperature critical assembly of the reflector-moderated circulating-fuel reactor was decreased from 0.416 (3% excess reactivity) to 0.345 g of U235 per cubic centimeter of fuel region, and a "clean" critical mass of 19.9 kg of U235 was obtained.
In one of the modifications, the average width of one of the end ducts was increased from 1.29 to 2.80 in., which increased its volume almost 2.5 times.
The corresponding critical mass was about 24 kg of U235.
In the other alteration, the radius at the center of the beryllium island was increased from 5.18 to 7.19 in.
The critical mass of this assembly was 18.4 kg of U235.
Another critical assembly of this reactor is being operated at temperatures between 1200 and $1350^{\circ}\mathsf{F}$ .
The minimum critical concentration has been determined as 6.29 wt $\%$ (2.96 mole $\%$ ) uranium in a mixture of sodium, zirconium, and enriched uranium fluorides.

Previous work on the RbF-ZrF $_4$ system had demonstrated the need for a fourth component in order to obtain a low-melting fuel with 20 to 40 mole% ZrF $_4$ .
All the work was carried out with RbF which contained about 20 mole% KF as an impurity.

Viscosity measurements in the system RbF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ (48-48-4 mole%) below $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ were hindered by particulate matter, but the measurements made above $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ gave viscosity values which were somewhat lower than those in the NaF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ system.

In order to obtain a melt with a kinematic viscosity as low as that of the ARE-type fuel, it was established that the BeF $_2$ content cannot be greater than 30 mole % to assure complexing of BeF $_2$ as BeF $_4$ .
The LiF content will probably have to be less than 10 mole %.
The melting point of NaF-LiF-BeF $_2$ (63.5-7.5-29 mole %) was found to be $525^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and a determination of its viscosity will be made in the near future.

The melting point of the 27-35-38 mole % mixture was found to be very low, $339^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , but its kinematic viscosity was 3.85 centistokes at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 1.47 centistokes at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Determinations were made of liquidus temperatures in the fuel system NaF-LiF-BeF $_2$ -UF $_4$ with 2.5 mole % UF $_4$ .
In these mixtures the concentration of uranium (in g/cm $^3$ ) at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is about the same as the concentration of NaF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ (50-46-4 mole %).
Liquidus temperatures were determined for mixtures of NaF-BeF $_2$ (70-30 mole %) with about 2.5 mole $\%$ UF $_4$ , because of the reported low viscosity of the NaF-BeF $_2$ mixture.
The liquidus temperatures were in the region of $560^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which is unsatisfactorily high.

Examinations were made of several Inconel forced-circulation loops operated with NaF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ (50-46-4 mole %) as the circulated fluid for various times under otherwise identical conditions.

These loops, which were direct-resistance heated, had a temperature differential of $200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , a Reynolds number of 10,000, a maximum fluid temperature of $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and a maximum wall temperature of 1600 to $1625^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
An attack rate of 3 to 4 mils per 1000 hr was found for the second stage of attack.

Another similar loop with a maximum wall temperature approximately $100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ higher than the usual 1600 to $1625^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ showed heavy subsurface-void attack to a depth of 18 mils.
In an all-stainless-steel loop in which sodium was circulated, there was a mass-transferred layer that was 0.8 mil
thick, in contrast to a 9-mil-thick layer found in an Inconel loop with a stainless steel cold leg.

Preliminary results indicate that the wall temperatures may have been as much as $1670^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , in contrast to wall temperatures of about $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in the forced-circulation loops.

The loop which circulated $\mathrm{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4}$ for 2000 hr with a positive charge applied to the hot leg showed only about one-half the depth of attack found in the loop operated with a negative charge applied to the hot leg.

A static test of a Hastelloy B specimen in an Inconel capsule containing a fluoride mixture showed the Hastelloy B to be unattacked, but the Inconel was attacked to a depth of 8 mils, in contrast to a normal attack to a depth of 2 mils in a static Inconel capsule without the Hastelloy B specimen.
Seesaw corrosion tests have been made on Inconel capsules loaded with sodium-potassium-lithium mixtures in which the lithium content was varied from 2 to $30\text{wt}\%$ .
The heaviest attack was found in the hot section of each tube, and it varied from 0.5 mil in the presence of $10\%$ lithium to 2 to 3 mils in the presence of $5\%$ lithium.
Molybdenum, vanadium, and niobium were tested in static lithium at $1500^{\circ}\text{F}$ .
Molybdenum was unattacked, niobium was only slightly attacked, and vanadium showed grain-boundary penetration by an unidentified phase to a depth of 2 mils.
Measurements of the oxidation rate of metallic sodium at 25 and $48^{\circ}C$ were extended to periods of $2\times 10^{4}$ min.

A series of studies of the four-component fuel NaF-LiF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ (22-37.5-35.2-5 mole%) exposed for 100 hr in sealed capsules of Inconel in the standard rocking furnace have indicated that this mixture may be less corrosive than others being considered.
Mechanical property investigations of Inconel have continued with stress-rupture tests of $3/4$ -in. tubing in argon and in fused salts at 1300, 1500, and $1650^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

A comparison of creep-rupture and tube-burst data showed similar rupture times for 0.060-in.-wall tubing and 0.060-in.-thick sheet in argon and in NaF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ (50-46-4 mole %) at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

Data obtained for tubing with 0.010-, 0.020-, and 0.040-in. walls show, in comparison with data for 0.060-in.-wall tubing, that the thinner the wall the shorter the rupture life at comparable stresses.

A series of creep-rupture tests of solution-annealed, 0.060-in.-thick, Hastelloy B sheet at 1500 and $1650^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in NaF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ (50-46-4 mole %) were completed and design curves were prepared.
The influence of aging heat treatments on the creep-rupture properties of Hastelloy B is being studied in the range 1300 to $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in argon.
The creep-rupture properties at $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ are not substantially affected by aging, since at this temperature a single-phase alloy exists.
However, at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ a second phase appears to exist in aged specimens which increases the rupture life at high stresses.

An increase in the reflector thickness from 8 to 12 in. decreased the sodium activation by $80\%$ .

Package 1 includes alterations and additions to Building 7503; construction of the 7503 cell, air duct, stack, adsorber tank, spectrometer facility, and fuel storage tank; a portion of the electrical power system; and all work required on roads, grounds, and fencing.
Package 2 consists in additional mechanical, electrical, and structural work for which design could not be completed for inclusion in package 1.
The work included in packages 1 and 2 is to be performed by an outside contractor.
The work items included in package 2 are the diesel-generator auxiliary power supply facility, auxiliary service and utility equipment, and lines up to the cell and to the vent-gas piping system.
Package 3 includes the installation, by ORNL forces, of the experimental instruments, controls, process lines, and process equipment.
Drawings and specifications for package 1 were prepared by the K-25 Engineering Division for lump-sum prime contracting.
All six prospective prime contractors who expressed prior interest submitted bids, which were opened August 16.
The bids ranged from $765,835 to$ 869,560, with bids for the cell included; the cell bids ranged from $229,000 to$ 280,000.
The contract was awarded on August 19 to the low total bidder, the V.

Provisions of the package 1 prime contract require that all work, except the cell and interior construction of the southwest corner of the building addition, be completed in 240 days, commencing August 29, 1955.
The cell and southwest corner installation are to be completed by June 1, 1956.

The ART will be made critical at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ with the control rod withdrawn about $1.5\%$ in $\Delta k$ from its fully inserted position.

The control rod calibration of the high-temperature critical experiment, carried to a point 4 in, above the mid-plane of the reactor, appears to be consistent with an over-all rod value of about $5\%$ in $\Delta k$ .

The initial rod position, of course, is, to some extent, at the discretion of the operators, and the increment in critical concentration could, if desired, be chosen to be less than $24\%$ - say 18 or $20\%$ - to correspond to an initial rod insertion of 2.5 to $3\%$ .

A critical assembly of the reflector-moderated circulating-fuel reactor loaded with sufficient $U^{235}$ to give about $3\%$ excess reactivity was described previously.

With the same structure and dimensions as those reported previously, the $U^{235}$ concentration was reduced from 0.416 to 0.345 g per cubic centimeter of fuel region, equivalent to a loading of 20.07 kg of $U^{235}$ , and the assembly (CA-21-2) was critical with only $0.14\%$ excess reactivity.
This assembly is compared in Table 3.1 with the assembly (CA-21-1) which had $3\%$ excess reactivity.

In one modification (assembly CA-22) the average width of one of the end ducts, the portion of the assembly that simulates the fuel flow channels into the reactor core, was increased from 1.29 to 2.80 in. to make its volume almost 2.5 times that of the originally constructed end duct.

The increased fuel capacity of the assembly necessitated the addition of $U^{235}$ in the end ducts, which made a total of $28.35\mathrm{kg}$ of $U^{235}$ at an average density of $0.405\mathrm{g}$ of $U^{235}$ per cubic centimeter of fuel material.

In another experiment (CA-23), with end ducts of the original size, the average radius of the central psuedo-spherical beryllium region or "island" was increased from 5.18 to 7.19 in., with a corresponding reduction in the fuel volume of from 58.2 to 44.3 liters.
The beryllium content of the core was increased from 67 to $92\mathrm{kg}$ including that in the end ducts.
This assembly was critical with a loading of $18.62\mathrm{kg}$ of $\mathsf{U}^{235}$ at an average density of $0.420\mathrm{g}$ of $\mathsf{U}^{235}$ per cubic centimeter of fuel region.
The excess reactivity was $0.19\%$ and the estimated critical mass, without control rods, was $18.4\mathrm{kg}$ of $\mathsf{U}^{235}$ .
The dimensions of assemblies CA-22 and CA-23 are given in Table 3.1, along with the data obtained with these assemblies.
The liquid fuel in the assembly is at $1200^{\circ}F$ .
It consists, essentially, of an annular fuel region separated from the beryllium island and reflector by $\frac{1}{8}$ -in.-thick Inconel core shells.

The System KF-ZrF<sub>4</sub>

Cooling curves obtained for ten stirred $\mathsf{KF - ZrF}_4$ melts with compositions in the range 33.3 to 59 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ were in general agreement with the phase diagram previously published.
Thermal breaks in the range 45 to 50 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ indicate that there are two forms of $\mathsf{KZrF}_5$ , one of which is metastable.
The System NaF-KF-ZrF<sub>4</sub>

Although the latter compound forms a compatibility triangle with NaF and K $_3$ ZrF $_7$ , it cannot form a quasi binary with K $_3$ ZrF $_7$ , Na $_3$ ZrF $_7$ , or NaF, because it apparently melts incongruity at about $755^{\circ}$ C to K $_3$ ZrF $_7$ and liquid.

Cooling curves on the Na $_3$ ZrF $_7$ -K $_3$ ZrF $_7$ join show a minimum in the liquidus temperatures at about $800^{\circ}$ C for the composition with about 15 mole % KF; the liquidus temperatures then rise gradually to about $918^{\circ}$ C for the compound K $_3$ ZrF $_7$ and about $850^{\circ}$ C for the compound Na $_3$ ZrF $_7$ .
Eight melts were made along the 33.3 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ join of compositions containing from 15 to 50 mole $\%$ KF and NaF as the remainder.

Although all these compositions contained the compound NaF.KF.ZrF $^4$ as one of the phases in the completely crystallized preparation, liquidus-temperature relations indicate that the compound melts incongruently and that therefore there is no quasi binary along this join.

Melting relations and phase compositions of six preparations along the 47 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ join indicate that the mixture with 47 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ also is not a quasi binary, because most of the compositions consist of three solid phases, including a new ternary compound of undetermined composition that is probably near NaF-2KF-3ZrF.
The minimum liquidus temperature along this join is about $420^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for a mixture with about 35 mole $\%$ KF.

A search in this general area has indicated that there is a eutectic, with the approximate composition 38 mole $\%$ KF, 21 mole $\%$ NaF, 41 mole $\%$ ZrF, which melts at about $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Thermal analysis data were obtained with a number of compositions within a wedge-shaped area in the ternary diagram having the $\mathrm{LiF - Na_2BeF_4}$ eutectic (16 mole $\%$ LiF) at the apex and $\mathrm{Na_2BeF_4}$ and $\mathrm{NaF - BeF_2}$ (70-30 mole $\%$ ) at the other corners.

The available data indicate that acceptably low melting points can be obtained with low LiF concentration by moving into the ternary system along the drainage path leading from the $\mathrm{NaF - Na_2BeF_4}$ eutectic (approximately 31 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{BeF_2}$ ; melting point, $570^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) toward the $\mathrm{LiF - Na_2BeF_4}$ eutectic.

Further, viscosity data for $\mathrm{BeF_2}$ -bearing mixtures indicate that, in order to obtain a melt with a kinematic viscosity as low as that of the ARE-type fuel (2.82 centistokes at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 1.34 centistokes at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ), the $\mathrm{BeF_2}$ content of the melt
must not be greater than 30 mole $\%$ to assure complexing of $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ as $\mathsf{BeF}_4 = -\mathsf{i}$ ions in the melt.

If an NaF-LiF-BeF2 mixture is desired, it also appears that the LiF content will probably have to be less than 10 mole $\%$ One composition that meets these requirements is the mixture NaF-LiF- $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ (63.5-7.5-29 mole $\%$ ), which has a melting point of about $525^{\circ}C.$ It is expected that the viscosity of this mixture will be determined in the near future.

The low-melting ternary mixture NaF-LiF-BeF $_2$ (27-35-38 mole %), reported previously on the basis of cooling curve data to melt at $325 \pm 10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , was found to melt at $339 \pm 5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ by the use of differential thermal analysis techniques.

This mixture has been reported to have kinematic viscosities that are too high - 3.85 centistokes at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 1.47 centistokes at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ - to be considered competitive with those of the ARE fuel but low enough to be of interest for a reactor design requiring a substantially lower melting point fuel than can be obtained with known $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ -base compositions.

Liquidus temperatures were determined for several compositions by differential thermal analysis of samples cooled rapidly from $675^{\circ}C$ to room temperature in order to avoid the segregation of the crystalline components that apparently occurs on slow cooling.
The composition NaF-LiF-BeF $_2$ $(50 - 16\%)$ showed a liquidus temperature of $478^{\circ}C$ and a solidus temperature, determined from heating curves, of $329^{\circ}C$ .
The mixture NaF-LiF-BeF $_2$ (64-5-31 mole %) showed a melting point of $545^{\circ}C$ and a freezing point of $555^{\circ}C$ .
An unpurified melt of this composition froze at $535^{\circ}C$ .

Most mixtures in this system have exhibited much lower freezing points than melting points because of the prevalence of undercooling, but the 64-5-31 mole % mixture appears to be exceptional in this respect.
The equimolar composition NaF-LiF-BeF $_2$ , previously investigated by Jahn, $^9$ was studied by using quenching and differential thermal analysis techniques.
The latter technique gave a value of $434^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for the liquidus temperature, as compared
with the approximate value of $460^{\circ}C$ reported by Jahn.

A crystalline phase having the x-ray pattern described by Jahn for the $\mathrm{NaLiBeF_4}$ compound prepared from aqueous solution was observed in samples of this composition quenched at temperatures ranging from 406 to $562^{\circ}C$ .

Jahn's failure to obtain $\mathrm{NaLiBeF_4}$ from melted samples by quenching at temperatures below $550^{\circ}C$ , which he ascribed to formation of $\mathrm{Be}_2\mathrm{F}_7^{3 - }$ ions in the liquid, was more likely due to poor quenching technique.
Solid-phase decomposition of $\mathrm{NaLiBeF_4}$ to give LiF and $\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{LiBe}_2\mathrm{F}_7$ at $225^{\circ}C$ , as reported by Jahn, was confirmed by the quenching experiments.

The filtration of the 27-35-38 mole $\%$ mixture showed that 9.65 wt $\%$ uranium, equivalent to about 1.8 mole $\%$ UF $_{4'}$ was in solution at $335^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Previous results<sup>9</sup> emphasized, contrary to generally accepted oxidation theory,<sup>10</sup> that sodium forms a highly protective oxide film in dry oxygen.

In this respect, additional evidence has been obtained in support of the idea expressed by Cabrera and Mott<sup>11</sup> that all metals have comparable oxidation mechanisms at relatively low temperatures.
The details of the experimental apparatus have already been described.[9]
During the period covered by this report, most of the experimental work has been devoted to the measurement of oxidation rates at 25 and $48^{\circ}C$ over extended periods of time.
At these two temperatures and also at $35^{\circ}C$ , experiments lasting between 15,000 and 20,000 min have been completed.
Seven rate determinations at $25^{\circ}C$ and four at $48^{\circ}C,$ all lasting for 17,000 to 20,000 min, have been completed.
Data from these, as well as the measurements obtained at $-79, -20,$ and $35^{\circ}C,$ are shown in Fig.
Each unit of the vertical scale is equivalent to approximately 10 Å of oxide; therefore after 20,000 min at $48^{\circ}C$ an oxide thickness of almost 1400 Å was attained.
5.16 represents the composite of data from three or more experiments.
Deviations of as much as $10\%$ in the total pressure change were observed between readings at the same time and temperature in different experiments.

Mathematical analyses of the rate curves show that none of the conventional oxidation-rate laws was obeyed by sodium; that is, the data could not be fitted to rate equations of the form $x^n = kt$ (where $x$ is the oxide thickness after a time $t$ , $k$ is the rate constant, and $n = 1, 2,$ or 3) nor to the logarithmic, $x = k \log t$ , or the inverse logarithmic, $\frac{1}{x} = A - B \ln t$ , rate equations.
